event: Samson Carries Away the Gates of Gaza
symbols: city gates — the strength and security of an enemy city, torn away by God's empowered servant
hilltop — Samson carries the gates to the top of the hill facing Hebron, symbolizing triumph elevated toward the Promised Land
moonlight and darkness — the nocturnal setting emphasizing Samson's solitary, divinely-aided feat against surrounding enemies
gates carried on shoulders — prefiguration of Christ carrying the cross and harrowing the gates of Hell
description: A solitary muscular figure ascends a steep hillside at night, bearing an enormous pair of city gates upon his shoulders. The gates, complete with posts and bar, form a massive dark silhouette against a moonlit sky. Rays of light break through clouds on the horizon. The surrounding landscape is rendered in Dore's characteristic fine engraving lines, with rocky terrain and a distant city below. The caption beneath reads 'Samson Carrying Away the Gates of Gaza.'
theological_description: Judges 16:1-3 records Samson's feat at Gaza: the Philistines lay in wait to kill him at dawn, but at midnight he rose, tore the city gates from their posts, and carried them to the top of a hill facing Hebron. This act of Spirit-empowered strength demonstrates that no human fortification can contain God's chosen deliverer. The Church Fathers (Augustine, Caesarius of Arles) read this episode as a striking type of Christ's Resurrection: just as Samson broke free at midnight, carrying the gates of the enemy city, so Christ burst forth from the sealed tomb at the dawn of Easter, shattering the gates of death and Hades (Matthew 28:2; Revelation 1:18). The direction toward Hebron — the city of Abraham and David — points toward the patriarchal promises and the Davidic covenant fulfilled in the Messiah. Samson's solitary triumph prefigures Christ who 'alone treads the winepress' (Isaiah 63:3), conquering death by His own divine power.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative: Samson tears away the gates of Gaza at midnight and carries them to a hilltop facing Hebron.
The Spirit of the Lord rushing upon Samson, the source of his supernatural strength throughout his career.
The angel rolling away the stone of Christ's tomb — Samson's breaking of gates prefigures Christ's breaking of death's seal.
Christ holding the keys of Death and Hades, the antitype of Samson carrying away the enemy's gates.
The solitary conqueror who treads the winepress alone, as Samson single-handedly overcame Gaza.
He breaks down gates of bronze and cuts through bars of iron — God's power to shatter every barrier.
God opening doors before His anointed and breaking gates of bronze, a prophetic echo of Samson's deed.

_meta.title: Samson Carries away the Gates of Gaza
